scenario: A team uses Camunda 8 SaaS. They wonder if SaaS-specific Connectors exist (e.g., to Camunda's own Operate, Tasklist, OAuth, etc.) that are unavailable / different in Self-Managed.
question: Are there Connectors specific to SaaS edition?

options.text: ) typically work identically in SaaS and Self-Managed. , to internal Operate / Tasklist) may have shared APIs but different deployment / auth (SaaS uses OAuth client credentials with managed Identity; Self-Managed uses your Identity / Keycloak). Functionality typically equivalent; setup details differ.

SaaS and Self-Managed Camunda 8 ship completely different Connector catalogs with minimal overlap; teams migrating between editions must rebuild their Connector configurations from scratch. This overstates the difference. The vast majority of Camunda 8 Connectors are edition-agnostic and work identically in both SaaS and Self-Managed. HTTP, Kafka, RabbitMQ, SMTP, Slack, AWS, and many other Connectors are available in both editions. The differences are primarily in authentication configuration and network access, not in Connector availability.

The SaaS and Self-Managed Connector catalogs are perfectly identical; every Connector works exactly the same way with no configuration differences between the two editions. While the catalog is largely equivalent, there are meaningful configuration differences. Secret management differs: SaaS uses the Console secrets UI, while Self-Managed typically uses environment variables or external secret managers. Network routing also differs: SaaS Connectors make outbound calls from Camunda's cloud infrastructure, while Self-Managed Connectors run within your own network.

Camunda 8 SaaS does not include any Connectors; the Connector feature is available only to Self-Managed users who deploy the Connector Runtime component in their own infrastructure. This misrepresents the documented behavior. Connectors are a first-class feature of Camunda 8 SaaS. The managed Connector Runtime is operated by Camunda in the SaaS environment, and the full out-of-the-box Connector catalog -- REST, Kafka, Slack, AWS, and many others -- is available to SaaS cluster users without any additional infrastructure setup.

a.text: Correct. Connectors in SaaS vs Self-Managed: Most Connectors are edition-agnostic: REST, GraphQL, SOAP: HTTP-based; same in both. Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT: message brokers; same in both. Email (SMTP, IMAP), Slack, Teams: communication; same in both. GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket: VCS; same in both. AWS Services (S3, Lambda, etc.), Google Cloud, Azure: cloud; same in both. Configuration differences: Secrets management: SaaS: secrets configured in cluster settings UI; referenced via {{secrets.NAME}}. Self-Managed: secrets typically environment variables or external secret managers. Network access: SaaS: outbound from Camunda's cloud to your services; firewall rules / IP allowlists needed. Self-Managed: same network as your services; direct access typically. Camunda-specific operations (e.g., from one process to query Operate): SaaS: typically OAuth client credentials via managed Identity; cluster endpoint URLs in cluster settings. Self-Managed: configured against your Identity / Keycloak; on-prem endpoints. Functionality equivalent; auth flow + endpoints differ. SaaS-exclusive features (verify per version): Web Modeler integration: deeper in SaaS (one-click deploys, etc.). Console: SaaS-specific control plane. AI Agent Connector: typically available in both, but credentials / billing differ. Self-Managed-exclusive considerations: Custom Connectors: deploy own packaged Connectors more easily (mount in Connector Runtime). SaaS: custom Connector deployment requires specific support / patterns. Practical advice: Develop edition-agnostic: avoid hard-coding edition-specific assumptions in BPMN. Use secrets: same {{secrets.NAME}} syntax works across editions. Test in target edition: networking + auth surprises common.
